So one quick thing transpired on a break and it reminded me of something I didn't say. So for our charities with our silent auction out there, first of all, whatever we wind up raising, I match. So it will double. And second, I thought, well, and although he would probably prefer not to be recognized, but Jay Geyer is pledged without taking any of the stuff, $10,000, as long as you all hit $10,000 by the end of the day on Friday. So if collectively you hit 10, he will give 10. And we split it, by the way, 50-50 with the two charities. And Jay has generously supported them in the past as well. In fact, we have the nice certificates that you gave me when you made those donations, framed and up in my office to sort of help people understand when they're there. So I am on your hijack 11, and this is not a page in your book, so I'm just talking. But so rituals, again, kind of underused in a lot of businesses. I early on saw it. So in a sort of a combination of a personal growth and get rich, some Trump U aspects to it, business, Vicki's uncle. So it was all ritual driven. Every weekend for a number of years, we had 500 to 1,000 people in a room for three days. The course was $5,000 in 1979. And you went up a ladder. So for example, everybody used green pens. You weren't allowed to write with anything but a green pen because green is the color of money. So we drove a lot of green flares being sold. Hard to buy them today, just a box of them. You got a special order. They got them in packs and you get one green and one red and one yellow and one purple. But at the time, you could go to the office store and buy a box of green flares. So everybody wrote with green pens. Everybody backed their car. You parked by backing your car. You never pulled your car into a parking space. And if you did, you got chewed out because you always want to start every journey going forward, not going backward. So every ritual has a reason. Everybody had to take notes at every meeting with carbon paper. Google it. I had, Dave, Brittany's last name, Lynch. So some of you know Brittany. She was in titanium for a couple of years. I like doing this every 10 minutes because I can see, it's only 16 people in a horseshoe. I can see the blank look on her face. And it's not because she's stupid. It's because I made some reference that predates her existence on earth like carbon paper. But everybody had to take their notes with carbon paper and turn their notes in and they had to write big, write small because you don't want to think small. We actually gave awards for the biggest writing. So there were all these rituals. Everybody had to carry $100 bills. If you got caught without a $100 bill, you got yelled at. You're supposed to leave the house every morning with hundreds. So there were these rituals. Like other repetitious behavior, if you can get people, obviously every religion has this, whatever religion you are a part of, every religion has this, if you can get people performing these rituals, it is easier to get them to do other things you want them to do because you've already established a pattern of behavior. You also create pain and disconnect, by the way, because it's emotional. It's not actual, but because if they're performing these rituals every day, they are less likely to leave you than if they are not. Symbols, number 12. So I saw it and I just saw it. I meant to tear it out. There's a book about Trump. It's not a flattering book. Somebody sent it, but somebody sent it to me. Anyway, there's pictures in the middle. There's eight or ten glossy pages with photographs on them. One of them is a very young Trump getting his first Cadillac. Many of you are old enough to know. Particularly post-World War II, all the way into the 1970s. The symbol that you had arrived, see Cadillac lost this, but it meant something then. The symbol that you had arrived, particularly for salesmen, was a Cadillac. And everybody knew it. And everybody understood it. And a ton of Cadillacs were sold because of their symbolic meaning, not because of what they were. My fight with James, with Ficky's uncle, was I hated him. So I had Lincoln's, town cars. And I'm the only one, because everybody else has got a Cadillac, because he came up with this. This is how Cadillac meant something to him, and Lincoln meant nothing. We had daily arguments about me trading in this car and getting a Cadillac, because you're supposed to have a Cadillac. So when things have symbolic value to the owner and in a community of owners, that can be pretty powerful. When you see a building, the architecture of which is Roman columns, what does that normally symbolize? Culture, knowledge, the law, and power. That's exactly right. Since the first time around, what did Obama's nomination and acceptance speech look like? He was Roman columns behind him. I presume Trump is going to have gold plated, Roman God knows. What does it instantly look like to everybody? The Bible. That's right. This is a book, by the way, about how to pick up women and get laid. That's what this is. So it's a fairly offensive appropriation to many people. Very deliberate though, because this is all symbolism, right? Of profound importance, of being the Bible of something. I'm all the way over now on your page 34, for those of you that are trailing along. Showmanship and optics. So Trump's really good at both of them. D. Verger used to work for Reagan. He was really good at optics. So first of all, with showmanship, there's something to be said for spectacle. So one of the things to remember about both of these things, optics and showmanship, is what I call, what does it say with the sound turned off? So this, by the way, is one of the ways we always look at video, at infomercials, is no sound. What impression do you get? Because most people, much of the time, they don't really hear what's being said. They certainly don't understand what's being said. So it is less about what is being said than the impression they get by what they see. Hence things like what car you arrive in, whether there's Roman columns or not Roman columns. All this stuff matters because it often is the way people actually judge your importance, your significance, your relevance, your relative power, all of that. So ever, and most people who had the experience of trying to put on a glove, that's a little snug. So it fits this using something very familiar in a demonstration to make a point. Right? It telegraphed the whole story. The story is cops corrupt, all the evidence is rigged, proves that story. So it fits the analogy criteria, and good demos often are really giant analogies acted out. That's what they are. So Tony's Firewalk, how many of you have done the Firewalk? Raise your hands. You learn more looking around than you do looking at me. Okay. Everybody know about the Firewalk. Right? Everybody knows the gig, right? So I for years have said that it's a circus trick from the 1930s, which it is, by the way. Okay? It was regularly done on the Carnival Circuit in the 1930s, mostly by a Firewalker. I mean, it is easier to run it as a trick when it's just you doing it than when you're getting everybody to do it. But it's still a complete trick. And it's easier to do the trick now than it was in 1930, because chemistry has evolved. The technology has evolved. There's more ways to make something seem burning hot that is not burning hot. But I am wrong. In doing the research for this, I have now traced it all the way back to 1907. There is a book. By the way, that book you sent me. We bought everything in the bibliography. Yeah.
